EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly buoyant and moist air mass resided over our area. The main upper level jet was over western Lake Erie, with a trailing upper level shortwave over southern Michigan during the mid afternoon. Both of these neared western New York by late afternoon into early evening. As these upper level features interacted with subtle lake breeze boundaries, there was a rapid increase in thunderstorm coverage across the Niagara Frontier. Storms blossomed across Niagara, Orleans, and Genesee counties. These storms rapidly became severe, producing some damaging winds and large hail. These storms all appeared to be rotating, helping them to maintain their severity for an extended amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was reported to have collapsed on Knowlesville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a strong cold front, storms along a prefrontal trough became severe. These storms grew quickly, with a line of convection over Canada reaching our western zones by late morning. Morning sunshine increased instability, especially along the warmer lakes. Broken lines of thunderstorms produced mainly wind damage across the area with two primary lines occurring. One of the lines was across the North Country, and the second was across the Genesee Valley. These two caused most of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down onto power lines near Jefferson and Eastview Terrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was in a weak cyclonic mid-level flow with subtle boundaries embedded in this flow. Limited shear was available while diurnal instability increased and supported thunderstorm development by midday. Storms developed along a mid-level boundary across western Lake Ontario and moved across the lake early in the afternoon. Outflow from these storms pushed across the lake and into western New York, weakening near Olcott. This outflow proceeded farther inland, continuing to weaken, with the exception of one location in Rochester where it intersected a developing thunderstorm and produced localized but significant damage. Storms also tracked up Lake Erie, producing gusts up to near 50 mph at Dunkirk and Fredonia. These weakened by the time they reached Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto Latta Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave moved east through the eastern Great Lakes, with a trailing weak surface trough and locally grown lake breeze and differential heating boundaries. An initial area of convection moved through the Niagara Frontier to western Lake Ontario in the morning, with additional development down the trough axis over Lake Erie and western New York. The somewhat early arrival of convection limited destabilization to some extent, but we still managed moderate instability by early to mid afternoon with a very moist environment. Low level winds were light, but somewhat better mid-level flow yielded moderate vertical wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several areas of weakening convection moved through the area during the morning. Following this, debris clouds slowly eroded and allowed moderate instability to develop. Remnant outflow boundaries and differential heating boundaries from areas of lingering cloud cover acted as the focus for new convective development in the afternoon, mainly from the western Finger Lakes to points southeast and east of Lake Ontario. Moderate instability coupled with limited shear produced a pulse storm environment, with brief, marginal severe structures. Storms were stronger and longer lived farther east in central and eastern New York where stronger instability developed. The pulse storms produced several instances of spotty wind damage. Convection briefly trained over eastern Ontario County, with numerous reports of flooded and closed roads. A little later in the evening, a short line segment briefly developed in the western Finger Lakes, along an old remnant outflow boundary from earlier upstream convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Street was reported flooded and impassible between Oak and Maple Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well ahead of an approaching cold front and more tied to convective enhanced shortwave, strong thunderstorms developed in clusters early morning. Warm rain processes dominated with precipitable water values closing in on 1.8 inches. Congealing storms dropped very heavy rain over north Buffalo to the Tonawandas. The rain occurred in span of 60-90 mins. As storms moved east, they progressed more quickly, so the main focus turned to strong winds once they moved into Ontario and Wayne counties and their adjoining waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 266 was closed from Sheridan Drive to Grand Island Boulevard due to flooding.

The rain occurred in span of 60-90 mins. As storms moved east, they progressed more quickly, so the main focus turned to strong winds once they moved into Ontario and Wayne counties and their adjoining waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 2.51 inches was reported in 60-90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and upper level trough moved through the region during the early afternoon and evening hours of Wednesday, August 7th, 2019. This brought scattered severe thunderstorms to eastern New York which resulted in numerous reports of trees and wires down.

A weak surface low across the Great Lakes on January 8th received upper atmospheric support (strong shortwave) during the night of the 8th and developed into a significant winter storm off the NH/ME coast by the morning of January 9th as it slowly moved into Newfoundland by January 10th.||Precipitation started as light rain, freezing rain and snow across northern NY during the evening of January 8th, changing to accumulating snow after midnight of January 9th. Snowfall was largely confined to mountain communities by midday of January 9th and continued until ending by late afternoon of January 10th.||Snowfall amounts varied greatly (3-12+ inches) with the highest amounts along the west-northwest facing higher elevation communities of the Adirondacks in northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration, elevation and upslope dependent snowfall occurred. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 18 inches, including 16 inches in Jericho and 4 inches in Plattsburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived storm system responsible for floods and heavy snows across California earlier in the week moved across the county reaching the lower Mississippi valley on Saturday, January 19th and just south of New England on Sunday the 20th.  Snow began during the afternoon of the 19th and ended by early afternoon on the 20th with snow accumulations of 8 to 16+ inches. Winds developed and increased to 10 to 20 mph with gusts in excess of 30 mph late Sunday through Monday causing considerable blowing and drifting of the snow.||Very cold temperatures were experienced Saturday through Monday as well. Daytime highs -5 to +10 with overnight lows 10 to 25 below zero. Strong winds late Sunday through Monday created wind chills of 20 to 40 below zero.||The combination of all these factors created hazardous travel through Tuesday as the very cold temperatures prohibited road chemicals to work effectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread snowfall of 10 to 20 inches occurred across Clinton county, some specifics include; 21 inches in Peru, 15 inches in Altona and 13 inches in Plattsburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved through the region ahead of a cold front. As a result of these storms, multiple trees and wires were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level shortwave, along with ample heat and humidity, resulted in numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of Saturday, August 17th, 2019. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a heavy snowfall event on January 19-20 over much of eastern New York, a strong low pressure system tracking through southern Canada ushered in an unseasonably warm and moist airmass on January 24th. Temperatures surged into the 40s to mid-50s. Steady rainfall occurred during much of the 24th as a secondary low pressure system developed over the Mid-Atlantic and tracked into southern New England. One to two inches of rainfall occurred over much of the region, although a few reports of three to four inches were recorded over portions of the southern Adirondacks and the eastern Catskills. New daily rainfall records were set at Albany, Glens Falls, and Poughkeepsie.||The combination of the rainfall along with the mild temperatures melting some of the snow resulted in urban and poor drainage flooding over portions of the region along with minor to moderate river flooding in the Hoosic basin. Some ice jam flooding also occurred. Numerous road closures also occurred due to flooding in Washington County, NY. In addition, seven vessels along the Hudson River near Troy were ripped from their moorings during the early morning of the 25th, likely by a surge of water and ice from upstream. Some of these vessels struck bridges, resulting in bridge closures during the morning rush hour, including the Patroon Island Bridge which carries I-90 over the Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 213 was closed between Creek Locks Road and Mountain Road due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front tracked through eastern New York during the afternoon of the 30th, accompanied by a heavy snow squall. Frigid air poured in behind this front along with strong westerly winds. Wind chills the morning of the 31st fell to 15 to 35 degrees below zero. The wind chills prompted many schools to close or delay opening, and there were several reports of water main breaks due to the cold.||In addition to the frigid temperatures, a heavy band of lake effect snow developed off of Lake Ontario and impacted far northern portions of Herkimer County from the evening of the 30th through the late evening of the 31st. Snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches occurred in this band.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly to northwesterly winds developed during the morning of the 1st behind a cold front. Several reports of wind gusts in the 45 to 55 mph range were recorded, with a few isolated gusts up to 60 mph. The winds brought down trees and wires and took out power to an estimated 25,000 to 30,000 customers. The winds diminished in the late afternoon.

Snow developed along a warm front and ahead of an upper level trough during the morning of the 29th. The snow was initially focused across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga Region during the morning, but spread throughout eastern New York during the afternoon and evening. The highest totals were across the southern Adirondacks, Lake George-Saratoga Region, eastern Catskills, and portions of the Mid-Hudson Valley, where 8 to 12 inches of snow was recorded. Amounts generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches across the rest of eastern New York. The snow resulted in schools being delayed or cancelled the next day along with several traffic accidents.

Following a heavy snowfall event on January 19-20 over much of eastern New York, a strong low pressure system tracking through southern Canada ushered in an unseasonably warm and moist airmass on January 24th. Temperatures surged into the 40s to mid-50s. Steady rainfall occurred during much of the 24th as a secondary low pressure system developed over the Mid-Atlantic and tracked into southern New England. One to two inches of rainfall occurred over much of the region, although a few reports of three to four inches were recorded over portions of the southern Adirondacks and the eastern Catskills. New daily rainfall records were set at Albany, Glens Falls, and Poughkeepsie.||The combination of the rainfall along with the mild temperatures melting some of the snow resulted in urban and poor drainage flooding over portions of the region along with minor to moderate river flooding in the Hoosic basin. Some ice jam flooding also occurred. Numerous road closures also occurred due to flooding in Washington County, NY. In addition, seven vessels along the Hudson River near Troy were ripped from their moorings during the early morning of the 25th, likely by a surge of water and ice from upstream. Some of these vessels struck bridges, resulting in bridge closures during the morning rush hour, including the Patroon Island Bridge which carries I-90 over the Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A residence on Ashgrove Road was evacuated due to flooding from an ice jam on White Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread eastern New York during the afternoon and evening of March 3rd ahead of a strengthening coastal low pressure system. While the heavier snow remained to the south, moderate accumulations of 4 to 6 inches occurred by the time the snow ended prior to sunrise on the 4th. The snow caused some schools to be delayed on the 4th.

Low pressure moved from the Mississippi River valley on February 12th into the northrn Great Lakes and Ontario Canada by the 13th. Meanwhile, a secondary low pressure system developed off the New Jersey coast on the night of the 12th and moved into the Gulf of Maine during the 13th.||Snow developed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of the 12th and changed to freezing rain at times across the southern Adirondacks before ending as snow on the 13th. A widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow occurred across northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across St, Lawrence county with freezing rain at times across the southern tier of St. Lawrence county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable air mass advected over all of western and north central New York ahead of an approaching cold front. Cloud cover earlier in the day and again in the afternoon from convective blow-off from storms over Pennsylvania helped coverage of the event be more scattered. However, where the tail of a shortwave and stronger shear to 30 knots occurred mainly east of Lake Ontario, widespread showers and thunderstorms occurred during the evening. Heavy rainfall also occurred, though there was just enough motion to the storms to keep the flash flooding threat lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to be down on Three Mile Point Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across the Great Lakes into Ontario and Quebec provinces on February 24-25th. Strong southeast down slope winds of 25-35 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph on the 24th caused some tree and utility line damage in northern NY and then strong southwest - west winds continued at 20 to 30 mph with gusts in excess of 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 24th switched to the west-southwest on the 25th...gusting up to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The day started with a very unstable air mass over the region but weak vertical shear. Extensive low-level stratus trapped beneath a 2,000 ft inversion kept low clouds around into early afternoon which, along with strong lake breeze off Lake Erie, tempered heating. Heat index readings did reach above 100 degrees Fahrenheit over Niagara and Orleans county toward Genesee River valley (less clouds), but they stayed lower than 95 degrees elsewhere. A mesoscale convective vortex moving in from northern lower Michigan and a well defined mesolow on radar brought a line of convection into the Niagara Peninsula around 12 PM. This convection reached the Niagara Frontier between 1 and 2 pm. While this more linear convection moved in, an additional isolated storm formed quickly farther east over Niagara county. Though shear was weak, strong instability led to the storm growing rapidly vertically. This storm produced wind damage with a tree down on house and multiple wires down. This isolated storm and the line of storms that moved into the Buffalo area then weakened while moving into rest of western New York toward 3 PM. As this occurred, generally non-severe convection developed over the Southern Tier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house with multiple reports of wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of oppressive heat and humidity, the atmosphere was ripe for thunderstorms. A mesoscale convective system (MCS) blossomed over Michigan during the afternoon hours and tracked eastward. It began to weaken while transitting Canada, but it still packed a punch as it reached far western New York. Even during the late evening, dewpoints in the lower 70s aided in producing a very unstable environment. Higher dewpoints were concentrated near Lake Erie and the Pennsylvania state line with another bubble near the western end of Lake Ontario. As this MCS drew closer to our region, it drew upon this instability and produced several thunderstorm line segments. These structures were greatest over Lake Erie and its shoreline, as well as Lake Ontario and in Niagara and Orleans counties. Well defined outflow boundaries were exhibited with these storms. As activity pushed inland, it weakened quickly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a garage on 80th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across the Great Lakes into Ontario and Quebec provinces on February 24-25th. Strong southeast down slope winds of 25-35 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph on the 24th caused some tree and utility line damage in northern NY and then strong southwest - west winds continued at 20 to 30 mph with gusts in excess of 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph were experienced with a measured wind gust of 55 mph in Malone with sporadic tree damage but there were reports of more damage elsewhere, thus estimated 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-level trough, low-level jet, and low-level boundary combined with typical lake breeze convergence zones and a modestly unstable environment to produce isolated strong to severe thunderstorms across many parts of the forecast area. Other than a half inch hail report in Chautauqua county, wind damage was the main report type in the Southern Tier storms, while a severe wind and isolated flash flooding were the report types with storms farther north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

Strong southeast down slope winds of 25-35 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph on the 24th caused some tree and utility line damage in northern NY and then strong southwest - west winds continued at 20 to 30 mph with gusts in excess of 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 24th switched to the west-southwest on the 25th...gusting up to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Within an extremely moist environment (precipitable water values in the vicinity of 2 inches) owing to the passing remnants of Tropical Storm Barry, the combination of daytime heating and a developing lake breeze boundary forced an initial line of showers and thunderstorms to form immediately to the lee of Lake Erie around mid afternoon. While this line contained torrential downpours, it moved quickly enough to prevent significant flooding concerns as it pushed east across Chautauqua and most of Cattaraugus counties. However, as this line reached far eastern Cattaraugus and western Allegany counties, it then merged with another line of heavy showers and storms moving north-northeastward from Pennsylvania, resulting in a west-to-east oriented line of heavy rain that became very stationary close to where a narrow swath of one half to one inch of rain fell the day before. Considering the antecedent conditions, the hilly terrain, one-hourly radar estimates of as much as 1-1.3 inches of rain, it was no surprise that flash flooding ensued in this small area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-op observer reported that 2.24 inches of rain fell in Oswego.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Mississippi River valley on February 12th into the northrn Great Lakes and Ontario Canada by the 13th. Meanwhile, a secondary low pressure system developed off the New Jersey coast on the night of the 12th and moved into the Gulf of Maine during the 13th.||Snow developed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of the 12th and changed to freezing rain at times across the southern Adirondacks before ending as snow on the 13th. A widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow occurred across northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across Franklin county with some freezing rain as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of oppressive heat and humidity, the atmosphere was ripe for thunderstorms. A mesoscale convective system (MCS) blossomed over Michigan during the afternoon hours and tracked eastward. It began to weaken while transitting Canada, but it still packed a punch as it reached far western New York. Even during the late evening, dewpoints in the lower 70s aided in producing a very unstable environment. Higher dewpoints were concentrated near Lake Erie and the Pennsylvania state line with another bubble near the western end of Lake Ontario. As this MCS drew closer to our region, it drew upon this instability and produced several thunderstorm line segments. These structures were greatest over Lake Erie and its shoreline, as well as Lake Ontario and in Niagara and Orleans counties. Well defined outflow boundaries were exhibited with these storms. As activity pushed inland, it weakened quickly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable air mass advected over all of western and north central New York ahead of an approaching cold front. Cloud cover earlier in the day and again in the afternoon from convective blow-off from storms over Pennsylvania helped coverage of the event be more scattered. However, where the tail of a shortwave and stronger shear to 30 knots occurred mainly east of Lake Ontario, widespread showers and thunderstorms occurred during the evening. Heavy rainfall also occurred, though there was just enough motion to the storms to keep the flash flooding threat lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak flow at all levels was present for Independence Day with weak upper level ridging across the mid-Atlantic and a weak trough across Ohio. Winds up to 700 mb were mainly less than 10 knots. A weak warm front meandered north and increased dewpoints and surface-based instability across western New York in the afternoon hours. Precipitable water values increased to nearly 2 inches on the warm side of the boundary. Storms developed along subtle boundaries in the mid-afternoon, but initially maintained enough movement to limit rainfall amounts. Then cells started to briefly back-build or organize into larger clusters late in the afternoon across southern Erie and Cattaraugus counties. Using spotter reports, it was realized that radar precipitation algorithms were underdone, and reports of 2 to 3 inches of rain were common in the flooding areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding prompted the closure of route 353 in Little Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest snowstorm to date in the 2018-19 winter occurred on January 19th and 20th across eastern New York. Low pressure formed over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted northeast across the Tennessee Valley during the night of the 19th before moving along the Mason-Dixon line on the 20th. Strong rising motion occurred as southerly winds aloft transported moist air northward, which was lifted over the cold airmass in place over eastern New York. Snowfall broke out during the afternoon of the 19th, continuing heavy at times into the evening and overnight hours before ending during the morning of the 20th. The strong southerly flow brought above-freezing air aloft into the region, causing the snow to turn to sleet as far north as the Capital District and northern Catskills for a time before turning back to snow. Further south, the air aloft was warm enough to turn the precipitation to freezing rain with ice accretion of one to three tenths of an inch over portions of the southeast Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics.||Snowfall totals were heaviest from the central Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga region where 1.5 to 2 foot totals were common. Three to six inches of snow and sleet along with some ice occurred over the Mid- Hudson Valley, while the remainder of the region saw between 6 and 18 inches.||The snowfall resulted in the cancellation of many weekend activities along with all flights from Albany International Airport scheduled to leave the morning and afternoon of the 20th. The governor of New York banned buses and tractor trailers from nearly the entire New York State Thruway and most interstate highways during the storm. States of emergency were declared for Albany and Saratoga Counties. Also, around 200 customers lost power due to the freezing rain in Ulster and Dutchess Counties. ||Frigid temperatures followed the snow for Monday and Tuesday, January 21st-22nd, with wind chills falling to -20 to -40F across most of the region. The cold weather prompted the closing of schools and the opening of warming shelters across the region.

Following a heavy snowfall event on January 19-20 over much of eastern New York, a strong low pressure system tracking through southern Canada ushered in an unseasonably warm and moist airmass on January 24th. Temperatures surged into the 40s to mid-50s. Steady rainfall occurred during much of the 24th as a secondary low pressure system developed over the Mid-Atlantic and tracked into southern New England. One to two inches of rainfall occurred over much of the region, although a few reports of three to four inches were recorded over portions of the southern Adirondacks and the eastern Catskills. New daily rainfall records were set at Albany, Glens Falls, and Poughkeepsie.||The combination of the rainfall along with the mild temperatures melting some of the snow resulted in urban and poor drainage flooding over portions of the region along with minor to moderate river flooding in the Hoosic basin. Some ice jam flooding also occurred. Numerous road closures also occurred due to flooding in Washington County, NY. In addition, seven vessels along the Hudson River near Troy were ripped from their moorings during the early morning of the 25th, likely by a surge of water and ice from upstream. Some of these vessels struck bridges, resulting in bridge closures during the morning rush hour, including the Patroon Island Bridge which carries I-90 over the Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9G was closed between Camp Creek Road and Maple Avenue due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of rain and freezing rain developed in the early morning hours of January 9th ahead of an occluded front. The mixed precipitation quickly changed to snow over the higher terrain as the front passed and cold air was drawn into eastern New York. Upslope snow continued over higher elevations through the day and into the night before ending on the morning of the 10th. Total accumulation over the southern Adirondacks and northern Taconics mainly ranged from two to six inches, with a few isolated higher amounts over northern Herkimer County. ||The air continued to become colder during the night of the 10th into the early morning of the 11th as strong westerly winds developed. This led to wind chill values falling to 20 to 25 degrees below zero over northern Herkimer County during the morning of the 11th.

A system tracked along the New York/Pennsylvania line and spread heavy snow across our region over the weekend. The low pressure track fit perfectly with climatology for widespread heavy snow in our area. The heaviest amounts that model solutions generally had across the western Southern Tier ended up being across the entirety of the Thruway corridor, resulting in over a foot of snow for much of the area. Northeasterly flow off of Lake Ontario resulted in localized maxima along the southwestern shore of the lake, as well. Even with more than a foot of snow, impacts were not as severe as they would otherwise have been because all of the snow fell over a weekend, resulting in lower impacts to transportation. Snow totals included 29.2 inches in Rochester, 13 inches in West Conesus, 14 inches in Sardinia, 17.5 inches in West Seneca, 18.5 inches in Forestville, 18 inches in Little Valley, 15 inches in Lowville, 14 inches in Warsaw, 16.8 inches in Lockport, 12.1 inches in Medina, 16 inches in Bristol Mountain, 12.9 inches in South Watertown, 26.6 inches in Walworth, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 10.2 inches in Alfred, and 15.0 inches in Pavilion.

A weak mid-level trough, low-level jet, and low-level boundary combined with typical lake breeze convergence zones and a modestly unstable environment to produce isolated strong to severe thunderstorms across many parts of the forecast area. Other than a half inch hail report in Chautauqua county, wind damage was the main report type in the Southern Tier storms, while a severe wind and isolated flash flooding were the report types with storms farther north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was reported down on McAndrews Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year was rung in by damaging wind gusts along the Lake Erie shoreline and across the Niagara Frontier and northern Finger Lakes. The early morning saw gusts reported up to 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front initiated a broken line of snow squalls to the region. The snow squalls developed in the afternoon across  the Lower Hudson Valley and then quickly moved east through the NYC metro and western Long Island during the evening commute. The snow squalls weakened as they moved across eastern Long Island. While snowfall accumulations were generally under an inch, the squalls brought strong winds and whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill during the squalls and created life-threatening travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls quickly moved through bringing whiteout conditions, strong winds, and dangerous driving conditions. The public measured 0.9 inches of snow nearby Manhasset Hills.

A mid level trough, upper level jet, and pre-frontal trough moved slowly east across the area. Western New York had clouds and showers early in the day, limiting destabilization. Sunshine lasted through midday east of Lake Ontario, allowing moderate instability to develop ahead of the arriving forcing. A broken line of convection developed over Cayuga and Oswego counties and slowly increased in intensity, becoming severe as it reached Lewis County. One area of the line bowed out as it moved across the Black River Valley. This produced multiple reports of trees and wires down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down onto Houseville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system tracked along the New York/Pennsylvania line and spread heavy snow across our region over the weekend. The low pressure track fit perfectly with climatology for widespread heavy snow in our area. The heaviest amounts that model solutions generally had across the western Southern Tier ended up being across the entirety of the Thruway corridor, resulting in over a foot of snow for much of the area. Northeasterly flow off of Lake Ontario resulted in localized maxima along the southwestern shore of the lake, as well. Even with more than a foot of snow, impacts were not as severe as they would otherwise have been because all of the snow fell over a weekend, resulting in lower impacts to transportation. Snow totals included 29.2 inches in Rochester, 13 inches in West Conesus, 14 inches in Sardinia, 17.5 inches in West Seneca, 18.5 inches in Forestville, 18 inches in Little Valley, 15 inches in Lowville, 14 inches in Warsaw, 16.8 inches in Lockport, 12.1 inches in Medina, 16 inches in Bristol Mountain, 12.9 inches in South Watertown, 26.6 inches in Walworth, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 10.2 inches in Alfred, and 15.0 inches in Pavilion.

Due to a warm January, very little ice cover existed on Lake Erie and by extension, Lake Ontario. While water temperatures were cold, open water combined with the influx of the first Arctic outbreak of the season combined with nearly stationary flow for two days yielded strong lake effect snow bands behind the passage of a cold front. Very gusty conditions allowed for these bands to develop localized blizzard conditions for large portions of the day in the Buffalo and Watertown areas before the wind settled down to just below blizzard criteria again. During the strongest portion of the event, wind gusts in heavy snow were reported up to 47 mph at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 45 mph in Wyoming, 40 mph at Niagara Falls Airport, and 43 mph at the Watertown Airport with consistent visibilities during a period around the highest gusts of one quarter of a mile or less as observed on webcams or automated observations. Total snowfall over the multi-day event was 37.6 inches in Watertown, 19 inches in Harrisville, 11.7 inches in North Boston, 20 inches in Attica, 14 inches in Corfu, and 21 inches in Buffalo. A massive snow removal effort was mobilized utilizing state resources from as far away as Long Island to cope with widespread blowing and drifting snow that made the already heavy snow amounts extreme difficult to deal with, particularly given that temperatures had fallen off toward 0 degrees by the end of the event.

Low pressure tracked from the coast of Maine during the afternoon of the 9th to the Maritimes of Canada by the afternoon of the 10th. A cold moist northwest flow of air crossed the relatively warm waters of Lake Ontario leading to heavy lake effect snow in north central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 7 and 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from Lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 19th to the northeast U.S. coast by the morning of the 20th. This low became a major winter storm which dumped between 8 and 15 inches of snow in central New York and between 4 and 10 inches of snow in northeast Pennsylvania. In addition, many areas of central New York and northeast Pennsylvania saw a period of freezing rain with up to a coating of ice in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches.

A weak mid-level trough, low-level jet, and low-level boundary combined with typical lake breeze convergence zones and a modestly unstable environment to produce isolated strong to severe thunderstorms across many parts of the forecast area. Other than a half inch hail report in Chautauqua county, wind damage was the main report type in the Southern Tier storms, while a severe wind and isolated flash flooding were the report types with storms farther north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down onto Randolph Road.

A sharp trough and associated clipper system crossed the eastern Great Lakes ahead of the development of lake effect bands of snow. Initially, bands developed northeast of the lakes just ahead of the clipper cold front early on January 25 and continued through January 26. Initially, the band off Lake Erie developed just north of Buffalo, then swept south into the northern portions of the Southern Tier. The band then backed with the flow back toward the Buffalo area and inland portions of Wyoming and Genesee counties. Off Lake Ontario, a band to developed north of Watertown then dropped southward toward the Tug Hill. Another band then developed along the south shore of the lake and focused on southeast corner of the lake. Snow totals included 20 inches at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 31.3 inches in North Boston, 15.8 inches in Wyoming, 14.9 inches in Perrysburg, 25.5 inches in Watertown, 13 inches in Silver Creek, 14.5 inches in Harrisville, and 7 inches in Batavia.

During the strongest portion of the event, wind gusts in heavy snow were reported up to 47 mph at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 45 mph in Wyoming, 40 mph at Niagara Falls Airport, and 43 mph at the Watertown Airport with consistent visibilities during a period around the highest gusts of one quarter of a mile or less as observed on webcams or automated observations. Low pressure tracked from Lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 19th to the northeast U.S. coast by the morning of the 20th. This low became a major winter storm which dumped between 8 and 15 inches of snow in central New York and between 4 and 10 inches of snow in northeast Pennsylvania. In addition, many areas of central New York and northeast Pennsylvania saw a period of freezing rain with up to a coating of ice in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 8 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front was draped across the region with lots of available instability but limited shear. Storms developed in the mid-afternoon, which prompted the first set of warnings. These first storms produced mostly sub-severe hail (up to three quarters of an inch). A second line then moved in with a bowing segment off Lake Erie, resulting in several wind damage reports. Storms became slower moving as the event dragged on, and reports of rainfall up to 2.5 inches prompted flash flood warnings over the Southern Tier, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Birch Run Road was flooded with water running over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp trough and associated clipper system crossed the eastern Great Lakes ahead of the development of lake effect bands of snow. Initially, bands developed northeast of the lakes just ahead of the clipper cold front early on January 25 and continued through January 26. Initially, the band off Lake Erie developed just north of Buffalo, then swept south into the northern portions of the Southern Tier. The band then backed with the flow back toward the Buffalo area and inland portions of Wyoming and Genesee counties. Off Lake Ontario, a band to developed north of Watertown then dropped southward toward the Tug Hill. Another band then developed along the south shore of the lake and focused on southeast corner of the lake. Snow totals included 20 inches at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 31.3 inches in North Boston, 15.8 inches in Wyoming, 14.9 inches in Perrysburg, 25.5 inches in Watertown, 13 inches in Silver Creek, 14.5 inches in Harrisville, and 7 inches in Batavia.

Due to a warm January, very little ice cover existed on Lake Erie and by extension, Lake Ontario. While water temperatures were cold, open water combined with the influx of the first Arctic outbreak of the season combined with nearly stationary flow for two days yielded strong lake effect snow bands behind the passage of a cold front. Very gusty conditions allowed for these bands to develop localized blizzard conditions for large portions of the day in the Buffalo and Watertown areas before the wind settled down to just below blizzard criteria again. During the strongest portion of the event, wind gusts in heavy snow were reported up to 47 mph at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 45 mph in Wyoming, 40 mph at Niagara Falls Airport, and 43 mph at the Watertown Airport with consistent visibilities during a period around the highest gusts of one quarter of a mile or less as observed on webcams or automated observations. Total snowfall over the multi-day event was 37.6 inches in Watertown, 19 inches in Harrisville, 11.7 inches in North Boston, 20 inches in Attica, 14 inches in Corfu, and 21 inches in Buffalo. A massive snow removal effort was mobilized utilizing state resources from as far away as Long Island to cope with widespread blowing and drifting snow that made the already heavy snow amounts extreme difficult to deal with, particularly given that temperatures had fallen off toward 0 degrees by the end of the event.

A weak surface low across the Great Lakes on January 8th received upper atmospheric support (strong shortwave) during the night of the 8th and developed into a significant winter storm off the NH/ME coast by the morning of January 9th as it slowly moved into Newfoundland by January 10th.||Precipitation started as light rain, freezing rain and snow across northern NY during the evening of January 8th, changing to accumulating snow after midnight of January 9th. Snowfall was largely confined to mountain communities by midday of January 9th and continued until ending by late afternoon of January 10th.||Snowfall amounts varied greatly (3-12+ inches) with the highest amounts along the west-northwest facing higher elevation communities of the Adirondacks in northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration, elevation and up slope dependent snowfall occurred. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 12 inches in the Adirondacks and 3 to 5 inches in the Champlain Valley.

A weak mid-level trough, low-level jet, and low-level boundary combined with typical lake breeze convergence zones and a modestly unstable environment to produce isolated strong to severe thunderstorms across many parts of the forecast area. Other than a half inch hail report in Chautauqua county, wind damage was the main report type in the Southern Tier storms, while a severe wind and isolated flash flooding were the report types with storms farther north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region and pushed a milder air mass that had resided across the area eastward, allowing for wintry weather to return. Temperatures behind the cold front at 850 hPa dropped to -12 to -14C over the open waters of the lakes that were around -3C. Layer moisture was sufficient for the formation of lake snow bands, which helped to overcome the shorter wind fetch of northwesterly flow. Snow along the passing cold front did not manage to account for much, however lake effect snow bands behind the front,  with upstream connections to the upper Great Lakes prompted warnings downwind of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. While northwest upslope flow resulted in largely synoptic snows over the North Country, snows from Oswego County to the south and west were largely lake effect. Snow totals included 13.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 9.5 inches in Sardinia, 22.5 inches in Celoron, 10 inches in Osceola, 9 inches in Port Byron, 7.4 inches in Macedon, 16.6 inches in Redfield, 7.6 inches in Wyoming, and 10 inches in West Carthage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind the front that caused widespread blowing and drifting snow across the area with localized blizzard conditions in Buffalo and Watertown, temperatures dipped below zero in the entirety of the area. This combined with wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph dropped wind chills substantially below zero. One homeless man died of exposure in Williamsville during the cold outbreak that closed almost all area schools and churches. Some of the recorded lowest wind chills during the period were -35 in Wellsville, -32 in Olean, -20 in Port Byron, -32 in Jamestown, -27 in Springville, -26 in Batavia, -26 in Calcium, -31 in Copenhagen, -28 in Livonia, -25 in Irondequoit, -30 in Niagara Falls, -35 in Wine Valley, -23 in Holley, -24 in Lacona, -24 in Newark, and -33 in Wyoming.

Deepening low pressure tracking from the southeast United States on Saturday January 19, 2019 to the northeast on Sunday January 20, 2019. An arctic air mass was located across New England  as the storm system approached the region. A warm front associated  with the low moved north along the coast early on the morning of January 20 changing moderate to locally heavy snow to sleet and then liquid precipitation. The warmer air battled the arctic air advecting into the Lower Hudson Valley from the north. However, temperatures remained below freezing through much of the winter storm across the Lower Hudson Valley creating a significant ice accretion on top of snow accumulation. Trees and power lines were brought down due to the ice along with power outages across many towns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, CoCoRaHS, and the public reported 4 to 6 inches of snow. A trained spotter also reported 0.4 inches of ice accretion in Newburgh, and the Montgomery Airport ASOS reported 0.27 inches of ice accretion. The combination of snow and ice created hazardous travel conditions along with downed tree limbs and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure tracked from the southeast United States on Saturday January 19, 2019 to the northeast on Sunday January 20, 2019. The low produced moderate coastal flooding along parts of the south shore of Long Island and New York City, and along western Long Island Sound, during the morning high tide cycle of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak water level of 6.5 ft MLLW occurred at the USGS tidal gauge at Hudson Bay at Freeport from 2019-01-20 06:48 EST to 2019-01-20 07:18 EST. The moderate coastal flood threshold of 6.2 ft MLLW was exceeded from 2019-01-20 06:12 EST to 2019-01-20 07:48 EST.||The moderate coastal flood threshold of 6.8 ft MLLW was touched at the USGS tidal gauge at Reynolds Channel from 2019-01-20 06:48 EST to 2019-01-20 06:54 EST, and again from 2019-01-20 07:12 EST to 2019-01-20 07:18 EST.

While northwest upslope flow resulted in largely synoptic snows over the North Country, snows from Oswego County to the south and west were largely lake effect. Snow totals included 13.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 9.5 inches in Sardinia, 22.5 inches in Celoron, 10 inches in Osceola, 9 inches in Port Byron, 7.4 inches in Macedon, 16.6 inches in Redfield, 7.6 inches in Wyoming, and 10 inches in West Carthage.

A weak warm front was draped across the region with lots of available instability but limited shear. Storms developed in the mid-afternoon, which prompted the first set of warnings. These first storms produced mostly sub-severe hail (up to three quarters of an inch). A second line then moved in with a bowing segment off Lake Erie, resulting in several wind damage reports. Storms became slower moving as the event dragged on, and reports of rainfall up to 2.5 inches prompted flash flood warnings over the Southern Tier, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported to close several roads in Lakewood, including Fairmont Avenue, 8th Street, Monroe Street, Chautauqua Mall Boulevard, and Route 394.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind the front that caused widespread blowing and drifting snow across the area with localized blizzard conditions in Buffalo and Watertown, temperatures dipped below zero in the entirety of the area. This combined with wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph dropped wind chills substantially below zero. One homeless man died of exposure in Williamsville during the cold outbreak that closed almost all area schools and churches. Some of the recorded lowest wind chills during the period were -35 in Wellsville, -32 in Olean, -20 in Port Byron, -32 in Jamestown, -27 in Springville, -26 in Batavia, -26 in Calcium, -31 in Copenhagen, -28 in Livonia, -25 in Irondequoit, -30 in Niagara Falls, -35 in Wine Valley, -23 in Holley, -24 in Lacona, -24 in Newark, and -33 in Wyoming.

Deepening low pressure tracked from the southeast United States on Saturday January 19, 2019 to the northeast on Sunday January 20, 2019. The low produced moderate coastal flooding along parts of the south shore of Long Island and New York City, and along western Long Island Sound, during the morning high tide cycle of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The moderate coastal flood threshold of 8.2 ft MLLW was touched at the USGS tidal gauge at Jamaica Bay from 2019-01-20 07:12 EST to 2019-01-20 07:36 EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak water level of 10.6 ft MLLW occurred at the NOS tidal gauge at Kings Point from 2019-01-20 10:12 to 2019-01-20 10:36. The moderate coastal flood threshold of 10.5 ft MLLW was exceeded from 2019-01-20 09:00 to 2019-01-20 10:48.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest snowstorm to date in the 2018-19 winter occurred on January 19th and 20th across eastern New York. Low pressure formed over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted northeast across the Tennessee Valley during the night of the 19th before moving along the Mason-Dixon line on the 20th. Strong rising motion occurred as southerly winds aloft transported moist air northward, which was lifted over the cold airmass in place over eastern New York. Snowfall broke out during the afternoon of the 19th, continuing heavy at times into the evening and overnight hours before ending during the morning of the 20th. The strong southerly flow brought above-freezing air aloft into the region, causing the snow to turn to sleet as far north as the Capital District and northern Catskills for a time before turning back to snow. Further south, the air aloft was warm enough to turn the precipitation to freezing rain with ice accretion of one to three tenths of an inch over portions of the southeast Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics.||Snowfall totals were heaviest from the central Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga region where 1.5 to 2 foot totals were common. Three to six inches of snow and sleet along with some ice occurred over the Mid- Hudson Valley, while the remainder of the region saw between 6 and 18 inches.||The snowfall resulted in the cancellation of many weekend activities along with all flights from Albany International Airport scheduled to leave the morning and afternoon of the 20th. The governor of New York banned buses and tractor trailers from nearly the entire New York State Thruway and most interstate highways during the storm. States of emergency were declared for Albany and Saratoga Counties. Also, around 200 customers lost power due to the freezing rain in Ulster and Dutchess Counties. ||Frigid temperatures followed the snow for Monday and Tuesday, January 21st-22nd, with wind chills falling to -20 to -40F across most of the region. The cold weather prompted the closing of schools and the opening of warming shelters across the region.

A cold front crossed the region and pushed a milder air mass that had resided across the area eastward, allowing for wintry weather to return. Temperatures behind the cold front at 850 hPa dropped to -12 to -14C over the open waters of the lakes that were around -3C. Layer moisture was sufficient for the formation of lake snow bands, which helped to overcome the shorter wind fetch of northwesterly flow. Snow along the passing cold front did not manage to account for much, however lake effect snow bands behind the front,  with upstream connections to the upper Great Lakes prompted warnings downwind of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. While northwest upslope flow resulted in largely synoptic snows over the North Country, snows from Oswego County to the south and west were largely lake effect. Snow totals included 13.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 9.5 inches in Sardinia, 22.5 inches in Celoron, 10 inches in Osceola, 9 inches in Port Byron, 7.4 inches in Macedon, 16.6 inches in Redfield, 7.6 inches in Wyoming, and 10 inches in West Carthage.

A major winter storm tracked from the eastern Great Lakes to northern New England during the morning of the 1st. This storm brought damaging wind gusts to Onondaga County during the morning hours. There was damage to the roofs of three buildings at the New York State Fairgrounds in Solvay, NY. The Syracuse Airport reported a maximum wind gust of 64 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked down trees and power lines in Onondaga County the morning of the 1st. The Syracuse airport recorded a maximum wind gust of 64 mph at 534 am EST. The winds caused a few roofs to be damaged at the New York State fairgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure tracked across the Great Lakes region during the evening of the 28th and the 29th.  This storm brought heavy snow to portions of central New York. Northern Oneida County and Sullivan County were hardest hit with between 6 and 14 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 8 to 14 inches.

A cold front crossed the region and pushed a milder air mass that had resided across the area eastward, allowing for wintry weather to return. Temperatures behind the cold front at 850 hPa dropped to -12 to -14C over the open waters of the lakes that were around -3C. Layer moisture was sufficient for the formation of lake snow bands, which helped to overcome the shorter wind fetch of northwesterly flow. Snow along the passing cold front did not manage to account for much, however lake effect snow bands behind the front,  with upstream connections to the upper Great Lakes prompted warnings downwind of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. While northwest upslope flow resulted in largely synoptic snows over the North Country, snows from Oswego County to the south and west were largely lake effect. Snow totals included 13.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 9.5 inches in Sardinia, 22.5 inches in Celoron, 10 inches in Osceola, 9 inches in Port Byron, 7.4 inches in Macedon, 16.6 inches in Redfield, 7.6 inches in Wyoming, and 10 inches in West Carthage.

A system tracked along the New York/Pennsylvania line and spread heavy snow across our region over the weekend. The low pressure track fit perfectly with climatology for widespread heavy snow in our area. The heaviest amounts that model solutions generally had across the western Southern Tier ended up being across the entirety of the Thruway corridor, resulting in over a foot of snow for much of the area. Northeasterly flow off of Lake Ontario resulted in localized maxima along the southwestern shore of the lake, as well. Even with more than a foot of snow, impacts were not as severe as they would otherwise have been because all of the snow fell over a weekend, resulting in lower impacts to transportation. Snow totals included 29.2 inches in Rochester, 13 inches in West Conesus, 14 inches in Sardinia, 17.5 inches in West Seneca, 18.5 inches in Forestville, 18 inches in Little Valley, 15 inches in Lowville, 14 inches in Warsaw, 16.8 inches in Lockport, 12.1 inches in Medina, 16 inches in Bristol Mountain, 12.9 inches in South Watertown, 26.6 inches in Walworth, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 10.2 inches in Alfred, and 15.0 inches in Pavilion.

Behind the front that caused widespread blowing and drifting snow across the area with localized blizzard conditions in Buffalo and Watertown, temperatures dipped below zero in the entirety of the area. This combined with wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph dropped wind chills substantially below zero. One homeless man died of exposure in Williamsville during the cold outbreak that closed almost all area schools and churches. Some of the recorded lowest wind chills during the period were -35 in Wellsville, -32 in Olean, -20 in Port Byron, -32 in Jamestown, -27 in Springville, -26 in Batavia, -26 in Calcium, -31 in Copenhagen, -28 in Livonia, -25 in Irondequoit, -30 in Niagara Falls, -35 in Wine Valley, -23 in Holley, -24 in Lacona, -24 in Newark, and -33 in Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought approximately 12 hours of light snow to portions of the Lower Hudson Valley on January 29, 2019. Temperatures at the beginning of the event were in the upper teens to low 20s and slowly warmed into the upper 20s by the end of the event. Snowfall rates were light throughout the event, generally a half inch or less an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, an emergency manager, social media, and the public reported 2 to 6 inches of snow.

The largest snowstorm to date in the 2018-19 winter occurred on January 19th and 20th across eastern New York. Low pressure formed over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted northeast across the Tennessee Valley during the night of the 19th before moving along the Mason-Dixon line on the 20th. Strong rising motion occurred as southerly winds aloft transported moist air northward, which was lifted over the cold airmass in place over eastern New York. Snowfall broke out during the afternoon of the 19th, continuing heavy at times into the evening and overnight hours before ending during the morning of the 20th. The strong southerly flow brought above-freezing air aloft into the region, causing the snow to turn to sleet as far north as the Capital District and northern Catskills for a time before turning back to snow. Further south, the air aloft was warm enough to turn the precipitation to freezing rain with ice accretion of one to three tenths of an inch over portions of the southeast Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics.||Snowfall totals were heaviest from the central Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga region where 1.5 to 2 foot totals were common. An arctic cold front tracked through eastern New York during the afternoon of the 30th, accompanied by a heavy snow squall. Frigid air poured in behind this front along with strong westerly winds. Wind chills the morning of the 31st fell to 15 to 35 degrees below zero. The wind chills prompted many schools to close or delay opening, and there were several reports of water main breaks due to the cold.||In addition to the frigid temperatures, a heavy band of lake effect snow developed off of Lake Ontario and impacted far northern portions of Herkimer County from the evening of the 30th through the late evening of the 31st. Snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches occurred in this band.

Strong westerly to northwesterly winds developed during the morning of the 1st behind a cold front. Several reports of wind gusts in the 45 to 55 mph range were recorded, with a few isolated gusts up to 60 mph. The winds brought down trees and wires and took out power to an estimated 25,000 to 30,000 customers. The winds diminished in the late afternoon.

Following a heavy snowfall event on January 19-20 over much of eastern New York, a strong low pressure system tracking through southern Canada ushered in an unseasonably warm and moist airmass on January 24th. Temperatures surged into the 40s to mid-50s. Steady rainfall occurred during much of the 24th as a secondary low pressure system developed over the Mid-Atlantic and tracked into southern New England. One to two inches of rainfall occurred over much of the region, although a few reports of three to four inches were recorded over portions of the southern Adirondacks and the eastern Catskills. New daily rainfall records were set at Albany, Glens Falls, and Poughkeepsie.||The combination of the rainfall along with the mild temperatures melting some of the snow resulted in urban and poor drainage flooding over portions of the region along with minor to moderate river flooding in the Hoosic basin. Some ice jam flooding also occurred. Numerous road closures also occurred due to flooding in Washington County, NY. In addition, seven vessels along the Hudson River near Troy were ripped from their moorings during the early morning of the 25th, likely by a surge of water and ice from upstream. Some of these vessels struck bridges, resulting in bridge closures during the morning rush hour, including the Patroon Island Bridge which carries I-90 over the Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surge of ice and water from the prior day's heavy rain and snowmelt moved down the Hudson River and tore a total of ten vessels lose from their moorings above the Federal Dam in Troy during the predawn hours of the 25th. The vessels included tugboats, barges, a cruise ship, and a floating restaurant. Some of the vessels struck bridges, some ran aground, and at least one sunk. The loose vessels prompted the closing of several bridges between Troy and Albany over the Hudson during the morning rush hour, including the Patroon Island Bridge that carries Interstate 90. Amtrak trains crossing the river were forced to reduce their speed as a precaution. The vessels were secured by 11 am. Monetary damage amounts were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system tracked along the New York/Pennsylvania line and spread heavy snow across our region over the weekend. The low pressure track fit perfectly with climatology for widespread heavy snow in our area. The heaviest amounts that model solutions generally had across the western Southern Tier ended up being across the entirety of the Thruway corridor, resulting in over a foot of snow for much of the area. Northeasterly flow off of Lake Ontario resulted in localized maxima along the southwestern shore of the lake, as well. Even with more than a foot of snow, impacts were not as severe as they would otherwise have been because all of the snow fell over a weekend, resulting in lower impacts to transportation. Snow totals included 29.2 inches in Rochester, 13 inches in West Conesus, 14 inches in Sardinia, 17.5 inches in West Seneca, 18.5 inches in Forestville, 18 inches in Little Valley, 15 inches in Lowville, 14 inches in Warsaw, 16.8 inches in Lockport, 12.1 inches in Medina, 16 inches in Bristol Mountain, 12.9 inches in South Watertown, 26.6 inches in Walworth, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 10.2 inches in Alfred, and 15.0 inches in Pavilion.

Snow totals included 29.2 inches in Rochester, 13 inches in West Conesus, 14 inches in Sardinia, 17.5 inches in West Seneca, 18.5 inches in Forestville, 18 inches in Little Valley, 15 inches in Lowville, 14 inches in Warsaw, 16.8 inches in Lockport, 12.1 inches in Medina, 16 inches in Bristol Mountain, 12.9 inches in South Watertown, 26.6 inches in Walworth, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 10.2 inches in Alfred, and 15.0 inches in Pavilion.

Behind the front that caused widespread blowing and drifting snow across the area with localized blizzard conditions in Buffalo and Watertown, temperatures dipped below zero in the entirety of the area. This combined with wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph dropped wind chills substantially below zero. One homeless man died of exposure in Williamsville during the cold outbreak that closed almost all area schools and churches. Some of the recorded lowest wind chills during the period were -35 in Wellsville, -32 in Olean, -20 in Port Byron, -32 in Jamestown, -27 in Springville, -26 in Batavia, -26 in Calcium, -31 in Copenhagen, -28 in Livonia, -25 in Irondequoit, -30 in Niagara Falls, -35 in Wine Valley, -23 in Holley, -24 in Lacona, -24 in Newark, and -33 in Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front initiated a broken line of snow squalls to the region. The snow squalls developed in the afternoon across  the Lower Hudson Valley and then quickly moved east through the NYC metro and western Long Island during the evening commute. The snow squalls weakened as they moved across eastern Long Island. While snowfall accumulations were generally under an inch, the squalls brought strong winds and whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill during the squalls and created life-threatening travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls quickly moved through bringing whiteout conditions, strong winds, and dangerous driving conditions. The nearby FAA Contract Observer at LaGuardia airport measured 0.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest snowstorm to date in the 2018-19 winter occurred on January 19th and 20th across eastern New York. Low pressure formed over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted northeast across the Tennessee Valley during the night of the 19th before moving along the Mason-Dixon line on the 20th. Strong rising motion occurred as southerly winds aloft transported moist air northward, which was lifted over the cold airmass in place over eastern New York. Snowfall broke out during the afternoon of the 19th, continuing heavy at times into the evening and overnight hours before ending during the morning of the 20th. The strong southerly flow brought above-freezing air aloft into the region, causing the snow to turn to sleet as far north as the Capital District and northern Catskills for a time before turning back to snow. Further south, the air aloft was warm enough to turn the precipitation to freezing rain with ice accretion of one to three tenths of an inch over portions of the southeast Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics.||Snowfall totals were heaviest from the central Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga region where 1.5 to 2 foot totals were common. Three to six inches of snow and sleet along with some ice occurred over the Mid- Hudson Valley, while the remainder of the region saw between 6 and 18 inches.||The snowfall resulted in the cancellation of many weekend activities along with all flights from Albany International Airport scheduled to leave the morning and afternoon of the 20th. The governor of New York banned buses and tractor trailers from nearly the entire New York State Thruway and most interstate highways during the storm. States of emergency were declared for Albany and Saratoga Counties. Also, around 200 customers lost power due to the freezing rain in Ulster and Dutchess Counties. ||Frigid temperatures followed the snow for Monday and Tuesday, January 21st-22nd, with wind chills falling to -20 to -40F across most of the region. The cold weather prompted the closing of schools and the opening of warming shelters across the region.

The strong southerly flow brought above-freezing air aloft into the region, causing the snow to turn to sleet as far north as the Capital District and northern Catskills for a time before turning back to snow. Further south, the air aloft was warm enough to turn the precipitation to freezing rain with ice accretion of one to three tenths of an inch over portions of the southeast Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics.||Snowfall totals were heaviest from the central Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga region where 1.5 to 2 foot totals were common. Three to six inches of snow and sleet along with some ice occurred over the Mid- Hudson Valley, while the remainder of the region saw between 6 and 18 inches.||The snowfall resulted in the cancellation of many weekend activities along with all flights from Albany International Airport scheduled to leave the morning and afternoon of the 20th. The governor of New York banned buses and tractor trailers from nearly the entire New York State Thruway and most interstate highways during the storm. States of emergency were declared for Albany and Saratoga Counties. Also, around 200 customers lost power due to the freezing rain in Ulster and Dutchess Counties. ||Frigid temperatures followed the snow for Monday and Tuesday, January 21st-22nd, with wind chills falling to -20 to -40F across most of the region. The cold weather prompted the closing of schools and the opening of warming shelters across the region.

The largest snowstorm to date in the 2018-19 winter occurred on January 19th and 20th across eastern New York. Low pressure formed over the lower Mississippi Valley and lifted northeast across the Tennessee Valley during the night of the 19th before moving along the Mason-Dixon line on the 20th. Strong rising motion occurred as southerly winds aloft transported moist air northward, which was lifted over the cold airmass in place over eastern New York. Snowfall broke out during the afternoon of the 19th, continuing heavy at times into the evening and overnight hours before ending during the morning of the 20th. The strong southerly flow brought above-freezing air aloft into the region, causing the snow to turn to sleet as far north as the Capital District and northern Catskills for a time before turning back to snow. Further south, the air aloft was warm enough to turn the precipitation to freezing rain with ice accretion of one to three tenths of an inch over portions of the southeast Catskills, Mid-Hudson Valley, and southern Taconics.||Snowfall totals were heaviest from the central Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga region where 1.5 to 2 foot totals were common. Three to six inches of snow and sleet along with some ice occurred over the Mid- Hudson Valley, while the remainder of the region saw between 6 and 18 inches.||The snowfall resulted in the cancellation of many weekend activities along with all flights from Albany International Airport scheduled to leave the morning and afternoon of the 20th. The governor of New York banned buses and tractor trailers from nearly the entire New York State Thruway and most interstate highways during the storm. States of emergency were declared for Albany and Saratoga Counties. The cold weather prompted the closing of schools and the opening of warming shelters across the region.

An arctic cold front tracked through eastern New York during the afternoon of the 30th, accompanied by a heavy snow squall. Frigid air poured in behind this front along with strong westerly winds. Wind chills the morning of the 31st fell to 15 to 35 degrees below zero. The wind chills prompted many schools to close or delay opening, and there were several reports of water main breaks due to the cold.||In addition to the frigid temperatures, a heavy band of lake effect snow developed off of Lake Ontario and impacted far northern portions of Herkimer County from the evening of the 30th through the late evening of the 31st. Snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches occurred in this band.

Following a heavy snowfall event on January 19-20 over much of eastern New York, a strong low pressure system tracking through southern Canada ushered in an unseasonably warm and moist airmass on January 24th. Temperatures surged into the 40s to mid-50s. Steady rainfall occurred during much of the 24th as a secondary low pressure system developed over the Mid-Atlantic and tracked into southern New England. One to two inches of rainfall occurred over much of the region, although a few reports of three to four inches were recorded over portions of the southern Adirondacks and the eastern Catskills. New daily rainfall records were set at Albany, Glens Falls, and Poughkeepsie.||The combination of the rainfall along with the mild temperatures melting some of the snow resulted in urban and poor drainage flooding over portions of the region along with minor to moderate river flooding in the Hoosic basin. Some ice jam flooding also occurred. Numerous road closures also occurred due to flooding in Washington County, NY. In addition, seven vessels along the Hudson River near Troy were ripped from their moorings during the early morning of the 25th, likely by a surge of water and ice from upstream. Some of these vessels struck bridges, resulting in bridge closures during the morning rush hour, including the Patroon Island Bridge which carries I-90 over the Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in the area of Midway Alley, Broadway, and West Main Street in Fonda.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surge of ice and water from the prior day's heavy rain and snowmelt moved down the Hudson River and tore a total of ten vessels lose from their moorings above the Federal Dam in Troy during the predawn hours of the 25th. The vessels included tugboats, barges, a cruise ship, and a floating restaurant. Some of the vessels struck bridges, some ran aground, and at least one sunk. The loose vessels prompted the closing of several bridges between Troy and Albany over the Hudson during the morning rush hour, including the Patroon Island Bridge that carries Interstate 90. Amtrak trains crossing the river were forced to reduce their speed as a precaution. The vessels were secured by 11 am. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system tracked along the New York/Pennsylvania line and spread heavy snow across our region over the weekend. The low pressure track fit perfectly with climatology for widespread heavy snow in our area. The heaviest amounts that model solutions generally had across the western Southern Tier ended up being across the entirety of the Thruway corridor, resulting in over a foot of snow for much of the area. Northeasterly flow off of Lake Ontario resulted in localized maxima along the southwestern shore of the lake, as well. Even with more than a foot of snow, impacts were not as severe as they would otherwise have been because all of the snow fell over a weekend, resulting in lower impacts to transportation. Snow totals included 29.2 inches in Rochester, 13 inches in West Conesus, 14 inches in Sardinia, 17.5 inches in West Seneca, 18.5 inches in Forestville, 18 inches in Little Valley, 15 inches in Lowville, 14 inches in Warsaw, 16.8 inches in Lockport, 12.1 inches in Medina, 16 inches in Bristol Mountain, 12.9 inches in South Watertown, 26.6 inches in Walworth, 20.6 inches in Minetto, 10.2 inches in Alfred, and 15.0 inches in Pavilion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front initiated a broken line of snow squalls to the region. The snow squalls developed in the afternoon across  the Lower Hudson Valley and then quickly moved east through the NYC metro and western Long Island during the evening commute. The snow squalls weakened as they moved across eastern Long Island. While snowfall accumulations were generally under an inch, the squalls brought strong winds and whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill during the squalls and created life-threatening travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls quickly moved through bringing whiteout conditions, strong winds, and dangerous driving conditions. The FAA Contract Observer at nearby Kennedy International airport measured 0.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic low pressure system tracked just to the north of the area with low CAPE but a strongly sheared environment developing in the warm sector of this system. This encompassed the southern portions of the area. Strong low level speed and direction shear was present with wind speeds up to 65 knots off the ground. A line of storms developed along a pre-frontal trough and moved across the region during the evening hours. Initial storms were further supported by a mid-level low that resulted in multiple hail reports. This area of storms evolved into a linear structure with multiple waves of stronger wind moving northward along the boundary. This line began producing wind damage with periods of diffuse rotation near these waves.

Though the primary west to east oriented frontal boundary with upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints streaming into it remained south across Ohio and Pennsylvania, a deepening low pressure system crossing New York State and a very moist air mass resulted in a dynamic moisture-laden system dropping heavy rain from the Southern Tier through Oswego County. The rainfall intensity was also enhanced by a mesolow that moved through simultaneously. Overall, multiple locations saw rainfall totals over 3 inches in less than 12 hours. Numerous road closures occurred during the event including both directions of the Thruway near Rochester. The flooding was so extensive that a State of Emergency was declared for the entire county on Thursday afternoon. Many flash flood and areal flood warnings were issued during this event and some of these persisted well into Friday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported near the intersection of Route 251 and 15A, closing both roads. Moving water was high enough to approach some home foundations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold advection allowed lake effect snow to develop off Lake Ontario. Light snow first developed across Jefferson County, then the snow bands intensified as they moved south of the Tug Hill. The steady motion of the band kept accumulations low until it stalled over Oswego County. Substantial blowing and drifting accompanied the heavy snows in Oswego County. Snowfall totals were around 17 inches in both Fulton and Oswego over the entirety of the event.

A strong and complex low moved out of the Midwest and into the central Great Lakes that passed just north of our area while secondary cyclogenesis occurred off the Middle Atlantic coast. The track of the low brought snow across the area that changed to mixed precipitation and eventually rain south of the Thruway. The warm nose aloft did not make it all the way up into the North Country, thus heavier snow fell there. This resulted in a longer duration synoptic snow that ended as a bit of lake effect off of the lakes, but due to ice cover on Lake Erie, the snow totals downstream of Lake Ontario were far greater. Additionally, in the wake of the cold front, gusty winds brought marginally strong winds into areas just east of Lake Erie. Snow totals included 15 inches in Constableville, 12 inches in Redfield, and 7.9 inches in Watertown. The strongest winds were back in Erie County, where 59 mph was measured at the Buffalo-Niagara International Airport.

A mid level trough, upper level jet, and pre-frontal trough moved slowly east across the area. Western New York had clouds and showers early in the day, limiting destabilization. Sunshine lasted through midday east of Lake Ontario, allowing moderate instability to develop ahead of the arriving forcing. A broken line of convection developed over Cayuga and Oswego counties and slowly increased in intensity, becoming severe as it reached Lewis County. One area of the line bowed out as it moved across the Black River Valley. This produced multiple reports of trees and wires down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down onto Erie Canal Road.

This encompassed the southern portions of the area. Strong low level speed and direction shear was present with wind speeds up to 65 knots off the ground. A line of storms developed along a pre-frontal trough and moved across the region during the evening hours. Initial storms were further supported by a mid-level low that resulted in multiple hail reports. This area of storms evolved into a linear structure with multiple waves of stronger wind moving northward along the boundary. This line began producing wind damage with periods of diffuse rotation near these waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Salamanca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pressed north across the forecast area during the morning and midday hours. In its wake, dewpoints jumped into the mid 60s while increasing amounts of sun allowed for a rapid increase in instability over the Southern Tier and Finger Lakes region. While the airmass destabilized, a low-level jet made its way across western New York. This supported ample deep layer shear. Initially, cells over the Southern Tier had trouble building due to the presence of a weak cap. Scattered storms over Allegany County finally broke that cap. Meanwhile, a strong lake breeze shielded the Niagara Frontier. A solid line of strong convection over southern Ontario approached the western edge of the lake breeze late in the afternoon. It was initially believed that the line would weaken as it tried to cross into the marine stabilized air. While the convection itself did weaken, a strong outflow pushed southeast across Niagara county. A warning was in place for the strong winds, and strong gusts did materialize in Niagara Falls. Additional cells formed in the still very unstable air mass over the Southern Tier while the southern Ontario convection broke down. As the evening wore on, the outflow from the southern Ontario convection merged with the eastern edge of the old lake breeze boundary, and a new line blew up east of Batavia and Warsaw. This line eventually supported warnings for the remainder of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down in New Hudson.
